Although no by-product is formed, a secondary reaction leads to hydrogen gas production. That is why it is recommended to wait at least 30 minutes rather than have a pitted cast. [19] 4. retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjacent Stainless Steel Crown With Arch Length Loss/Space Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of the crown's mesiodistal dimension is frequently required, particularly when mesial drift (often caused by caries) has resulted in arch length loss. Flattening the crown's mesial and distal contact areas using Adam pliers results in a moderate reduction in the mesiodistal dimension. A contralateral upper tooth crown with a smaller size can be used if there is mesial drift in the lower arch. Another form of modification is vertical slicing and spot-welding, an additional segment of a stainless steel band. This is done to increase the perimeter or extend the length. However, the efficacy of the latter modifications remains largely untested. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Skin Grafting(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- Full-Thickness Skin Graft
retrieved_docs.content: After selection of a donor site, both sites are sterilely prepped, draped, and anesthetized. A template of the defect can be made using a gauze, measurements or foil from the suture packaging. This template is then transposed onto the donor site. Classically, the donor site should be closed with a length to width ratio of 3:1, but Wang et al. [7] suggested that this ratio may not be necessary for donor sites, which could leave smaller donor defects. Some debate exists in the literature on the proper sizing of the graft. In general, grafts are oversized by 10% to 20% to allow for contraction and to allow contouring of the graft. [8] However, some others have suggested that grafts should be undersized by up to 10% to 20% to prevent puckering or pincushioning of the graft. [9] This suggests that there is leeway in terms of graft size, especially when grafts that are too small can be meshed to increase the size of the graft. FTSG can be harvested using a standard excision technique. After the graft is removed, it is placed in sterile saline while hemostasis is achieved at the donor site. The graft is subsequently defatted using a scalpel or scissors. An alternative method would be to take the deep margin of the graft just above the subcutaneous fat, which eliminates the defatting step. The graft should be contoured to the size of the defect and placed in the wound bed as quickly as possible. It is imperative that the graft is placed in apposition to the wound bed to reduce the risk of graft failure. Typically, grafts are sutured with a quickly absorbing suture, such as chromic gut or a non-absorbable suture such as nylon. For large grafts or at the preference of the surgeon, basting sutures can be placed. Basting suture should be placed first to allow for hemostasis of bleeding induced by suture placement. After hemostasis of the wound bed is achieved, the graft edges are sutured into place with an emphasis placed on apposition of the wound bed and graft. After the graft is sutured in place, a bolster is placed over the graft to further assist in apposition. A variety of different products are available to use as bolsters, such as petroleum impregnated gauze. The bolster and overlying pressure dressing are used to secure the graft in place and to prevent desiccation of the graft. The bolster and pressure dressing can be removed in 1 week followed by removal of nonabsorbable sutures, if they are used, in approximately 2 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertebral Augmentation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous kyphoplasty Percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP), a technique designed for fracture reduction, vertebral height restoration, and kyphosis correction, was introduced nearly a decade after the advent of PVP. [3] [5] PKP facilitates the injection of PMMA or mineralized collagen-modified PMMA into the created cavity under low pressure, effectively minimizing the risk of cement leakage (1%–8% in PKP vs 30% in PVP). [2] In this procedure, a balloon or bone tamp is percutaneously introduced under image guidance through the pedicle into the vertebral body and inflated to establish a cavity, simultaneously elevating the endplates. Thicker, partially cured cement with high viscosity is injected into this closed cavity at low velocity. While the bipedicular approach was initially commonplace, the current trend favors the unipedicular approach due to reduced operative time, lower cement volume, and diminished radiation hazards. A meta-analysis has demonstrated no differences in pain, functional outcomes, or the risk of cement leakage between the 2 approaches. [2] The expedient elevation of the end plate and restoration of vertebral body height within 1 month characterizes the efficacy of this procedure. Some proponents recommend performing VA within 10 days before the impending fracture impaction to prevent deformity. Kyphoplasty also offers the advantage of providing an option for biopsy. However, drawbacks include increased costs, exposure to contrast agents, and the requirement for general anesthesia. Compared to vertebroplasty, kyphoplasty is typically executed via a bipedicular approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertebral Augmentation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Vertebral body stenting The vertebral body stenting system involves inserting a balloon-expandable metal stent mounted on a balloon catheter into the vertebral body. Two stents are inserted bilaterally and inflated with a contrast-saline solution under pressures up to 30 atm. This symmetrically expands both stents. The stent implants are made of a strong and ductile cobalt-chromium alloy commonly used in coronary and peripheral artery stenting. The unexpanded stent comes precrimped on the balloon and gradually expands to its final large-diameter configuration. This expansion is facilitated by its laser-cut mesh pattern, where individual one-fourth by one-half mm-thick struts keep spreading apart until fracture reduction or the maximum diameter of 17 mm is reached. Once the balloon-assisted stent expansion is complete, the balloons are deflated and retrieved, leaving the stents in place to maintain the restored height. PMMA cement is injected into the cavities supported by the stent mesh structures to produce a stent-reinforced cement implant within the treated vertebral body. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation
retrieved_docs.content: Operators can use the number 137 pliers can be used for circumferential contouring. Contouring pliers deliver a belling effect in the middle third of the crown. [19] Johnson contouring pliers with a No. 114 are used for cervical crown adaptation on the cervical third of the buccal and lingual surfaces. A curved beak plier No. 114 is then used to contour the buccal and lingual surfaces. Curved beak pliers may also contour the crown's proximal areas. [20] [13] Lastly, the crimping pliers are used to crimp the cervical margin 1 mm circumferentially for final adaptation and ensure that the crown margin's outline follows the gingival margin of the tooth. The operators can check the final adaptation of the crown by taking a radiograph to verify the gingival contour and extension, ensuring the complete coverage of the tooth. [13] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Preventive and reparative measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” “Trochanterplasty” reshapes the greater trochanter to improve hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip

retrieved_docs.content: of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation.” Osteoplastic procedures modify femoral length or contour and include “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and the combined “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer.” Preventive and reconstructive measures are captured by “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck,” “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur” (soft‑tissue repair). Joint‑related reconstruction is addressed by “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” while “Trochanterplasty” reshapes or repositions the greater trochanter. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus. Reconstructive

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: and repair of malunion distal to the femoral head and neck. Procedures that modify femoral length or geometry are classified as having a procedural site rather than a direct site and comprise osteoplasty of the femur for lengthening, osteoplasty of the femur for shortening, and combined lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer, which are employed to correct limb‑length discrepancy and angular deformities. Indirect femoral interventions include replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate, revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), and prophylactic treatment of the femur; these are performed as part of broader reconstructive or prosthetic strategies rather than primary bone fixation. Growth‑modulating procedures linked to the femur through a “has relationship” connection are epiphyseal arrest (any method, e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia or fibula, which are used to halt

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: pathology or correct malunited fractures, while “Suture of periosteum of femur” supports periosteal integrity for bone healing. Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” and “Trochanterplasty,” which address femoral implant revision, comprehensive knee arthroplasty component exchange, and greater trochanter reshaping to improve hip biomechanics. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the extensive, femur‑centered surgical repertoire for managing growth disturbances, fractures, malunions, deformities, and prosthetic complications, emphasizing the need for individualized operative planning based on anatomical site, underlying pathology, and desired functional outcome.

Collectively, this network of femoral‑focused and lower‑extremity surgical options provides clinicians with comprehensive strategies for fracture stabilization, malunion correction, growth disturbance management, limb‑length modification, prosthetic revision, joint reconstruction, and soft‑tissue repair, thereby supporting optimal functional outcomes across a spectrum of orthopedic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1827
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a therapeutic formulation that incorporates kalcium-glycerolfosfát and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components. Kalcium-glycerolfosfát provides a calcium‑phosphate source that can support mineralization processes, while sodium hyaluronate contributes viscoelastic and lubricating properties useful for tissue repair and joint function. By combining these two agents, the replacement preparation is designed to enhance clinical outcomes in procedures that require both structural support and improved biomechanical performance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.
